content.full_text:
David M. Kennedy 
City Attorney 
200 Lincoln Avenue 
Salinas, CA 
93901 
Dear Mr. Kennedy: 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322·5662 
Administratian 
322·5660 
Executive/legal 
322·5901 
April 23, 1985 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Re: 
Your Request for Advice 
Our File No. A-85-087 
Thank you for your letter requesting advice about the 
conflict of interest provisions of the Political Reform Act. 
You have referred to our letter to Robert B. Yee, File No. 
A-85-323 (copy enclosed), in which we stated that city employee 
who holas a salaried position as executive director of a 
nonprofit community agency must disqualify himself from 
participating as a city employee in any decision that would 
affect grants to the community agency, when the decisions are 
made by his department, and must also refrain from attempting to 
influence decisions of his department affecting the community 
agency's application for funds. 
You have asked whether the same 
restrictions would apply to a city employee who is an unsalaried 
executive director of a nonprofit community agency. 
I have 
enclosed a copy of a letter to Sidney Maleck of El Monte, File 
No. A-80-03l, which addresses a similar question. 
If, after reading the enclosed letters, you have any 
further questions about a specific decision or a specific fact 
situation, please contact me at (916) 322-5901. 
KED:plh 
Enclosures 
Very truly yours, 
(Ktrt~,- £ .~C/--'V-rl/l-'--
Kathryn E. Donovan 
Counsel 
Legal Division 


April 11, 1985 
FPPC 
1100 K Street 
Sacramento, CA 
95814 
Gentlemen: 
Please refer to your letter to Robert B. Yee, Oakland, dated 
January 25, 1985, File No. A-84-323, as summarized in Vol. 11, 
No.4. 
Would your advice be any different if the City employee were 
a nonsalaried board of director member of such a nonprofit 
agency? 
Very truly yours, 
DAVID M. KENNEDY 
City Attorney 
DMK: jb 
cc: 
Ed Piper 


P.O. BOX 807 • SACRAMENTO, 95804 ••• 1100 K STREET BUILDING, SACRAMENTO, 95814 
Technical Assistance 
(916) 322-5662 
Administratian 
322·5660 
April 17, 1985 
David M. Kennedy 
Salinas City Attorney 
200 Lincoln Avenue 
Salinas, CA 
93901 
Dear Mr. Kennedy: 
Re: 
A-85-087 
Executive/legal 
322.5901 
Enforcement 
322-6441 
Your letter requesting advice under the Political 
Reform Act has been referred to Kathryn Donovan, an 
attorney in the Legal Division of the Fair Political 
Practices Commission. 
If you have any questions about your 
advice request, you may contact this attorney directly at 
(916) 322-5901. 
We try to answer all advice requests promptly. 
Therefore, unless your request poses particularly complex 
legal questions, or unless more information is needed to 
answer your request, you should expect a response within 21 
working days. 
BAM:plh 
Very truly yours, 
Barbara A. Milman 
General Counsel

sections.question: Would the restrictions on a city employee holding a salaried position as executive director of a nonprofit community agency (disqualification from decisions affecting grants and refraining from attempting to influence decisions affecting the agency's fund applications) also apply to a city employee who is an unsalaried executive director of a nonprofit community agency? More specifically, would the advice be any different if the city employee were a nonsalaried board of director member of such a nonprofit agency?
sections.conclusion: The FPPC advises that the same restrictions would apply to a city employee who is an unsalaried executive director of a nonprofit community agency. The FPPC references prior letters (and ) that address similar questions and directs the City Attorney to review those letters, noting that specific restrictions regarding disqualification and refraining from attempting to influence departmental decisions affecting the agency would apply in this situation as well.
sections.question_synthetic: The City Attorney asks whether conflict of interest restrictions that apply to a salaried city employee serving as executive director of a nonprofit agency would also apply to an unsalaried city employee holding similar positions.
sections.conclusion_synthetic: The FPPC confirms that the conflict of interest restrictions applicable to salaried positions also apply to unsalaried executive director or board member positions with nonprofit agencies, requiring disqualification from related decisions and prohibition on attempting to influence such decisions.
sections.parsing_notes: No section headers found; LLM: Document contains the incoming request dated April 11, 1985 from David Kennedy, an April 17 acknowledgment letter, and the April 23 substantive advice response from FPPC counsel Kathryn Donovan. The conclusion is somewhat indirect as it references prior letters rather than providing a fully detailed answer within this letter itself. The answer indicates the same restrictions apply but directs the requestor to review other enclosed correspondence for full reasoning.

embedding.qa_text:
QUESTION: Would the restrictions on a city employee holding a salaried position as executive director of a nonprofit community agency (disqualification from decisions affecting grants and refraining from attempting to influence decisions affecting the agency's fund applications) also apply to a city employee who is an unsalaried executive director of a nonprofit community agency? More specifically, would the advice be any different if the city employee were a nonsalaried board of director member of such a nonprofit agency?

CONCLUSION: The FPPC advises that the same restrictions would apply to a city employee who is an unsalaried executive director of a nonprofit community agency. The FPPC references prior letters (and ) that address similar questions and directs the City Attorney to review those letters, noting that specific restrictions regarding disqualification and refraining from attempting to influence departmental decisions affecting the agency would apply in this situation as well.

embedding.summary: The FPPC responds to the City Attorney's request for advice regarding whether conflict of interest provisions restricting a salaried city employee's involvement with a nonprofit community agency also apply to unsalaried positions, directing him to prior advisory letters that address the same issue.
